planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in sharply drained, lean, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), phosphorus-poor soil in full sun; king protea is fatally sensitive to phosphorus toxicity — never use standard garden fertilizers or composts with phosphate. Use only protea-specific or native plant (Proteaceae) fertilizer. Excellent drainage is non-negotiable; wet feet are the primary cause of death.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, acidic, phosphorus-poor soil in full sun; king protea is South Africa's national flower — the most iconic member of the Proteaceae family and the largest protea species — producing extraordinary globe-shaped flower heads 8–12 inches across with ring upon ring of petal-like bracts in shades of pale silvery-pink to deep rose-crimson surrounding a central dome of silvery-gray florets, the whole resembling a vast artichoke or a jeweled crown. Blooms appear in late winter through summer on plants typically 3–7 feet tall and nearly as wide. It is the dominant species in the South African fynbos (heathland) and requires the same conditions — excellent drainage, lean, low-fertility, low-phosphorus, slightly acidic soil, full sun, and Mediterranean-style dry summers with winter moisture. Phosphorus toxicity from ordinary fertilizers or manure kills it rapidly. Outstanding as a cut flower of extraordinary longevity (lasts 2–3 weeks in a vase). Succeeds in California, coastal Southern California, and similar Mediterranean-climate warm-winter gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in sharply drained, lean, low-phosphorus, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; coast banksia is salt- and wind-tolerant and one of the hardiest banksias, surviving brief temperatures to 23°F (zone 8). Like all Proteaceae, it is severely damaged or killed by standard fertilizers with phosphate. Excellent drainage is essential.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, low-phosphorus, slightly acidic soil in full sun; coast banksia is one of the most adaptable and cold-hardy Australian banksias, a coastal tree of eastern Australia growing 10–30 feet with distinctive pale-green leaves that are dark green above and silvery-white below — creating a two-toned effect as leaves move in the wind. The characteristic cylindrical banksia flower spikes (4–6 inches long) are produced primarily in autumn and winter in soft, pale-yellow, and are among the richest natural nectar sources of any flowering tree — overwhelming in the volume and concentration of the nectar they produce. This makes them critical wildlife habitat for honeyeaters, lorikeets, bats, and a vast array of insects including native bees. The hardened, woody seed cones that follow the flowers can persist on the branch for years before opening to release seeds in response to fire (a key ecological adaptation). One of the most cold-tolerant banksias and the best starting species for gardens in zones 8b–11 seeking Australian native character.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in sharply drained, lean, phosphorus-poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rosemary grevillea requires excellent drainage above all else — wet, heavy clay soils or overwatering are the primary causes of failure. Use low-phosphorus fertilizer only. It is moderately frost-tolerant once established, surviving to 15–20°F in zone 8.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, low-phosphorus, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rosemary grevillea is an Australian shrub named for its narrow, rosemary-like needle leaves (prickly to the touch) on arching, graceful stems that form a loose, airy, medium-sized shrub 3–6 feet tall. From late autumn through spring it produces clusters of spidery, deep-pink to crimson-red flowers in the distinctive grevillea style — curved, nectar-rich, toothbrush-shaped clusters that are irresistible to hummingbirds and honey-producing bees, providing critical winter nectar during the period when almost no other nectar source flowers in California and the Southwest. One of the most consistently winter-flowering garden shrubs in zones 8b–10. Drought-tolerant once established (requires no summer water in Mediterranean climates), long-lived, and largely pest- and disease-free in appropriate conditions. An essential plant for California native wildlife gardens and for year-round hummingbird habitat.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; transplant to part shade after frosts. Chinese foxglove self-sows modestly in mild climates and is short-lived but reseeds to maintain a colony. In zones 8–9 it often overwinters as a basal rosette and reblooms the following year.

directions: Start seeds indoors and transplant to moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade; Chinese foxglove is an elegant short-lived perennial from China producing a basal rosette of deeply lobed, somewhat sticky, aromatic leaves and upright stems bearing a succession of large (2-inch), tubular, rose-pink to rose-purple flowers with yellow, spotted throats — extremely similar to foxglove in form but in warm, rosy-purple tones rather than the usual foxglove purples and whites. The flowers appear over a long season from late spring through midsummer and are attractive to bees. A related species, R. glutinosa, provides di huang — one of the most important herbs in traditional Chinese medicine, used for thousands of years to treat blood-deficiency conditions. R. elata is the ornamental species, valued for its shade tolerance, its foxglove-like effect in the garden, and its combination of interesting, textured foliage with refined, warm-colored flowers in situations where foxglove itself would prefer cooler conditions.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in part shade; Canary Island foxglove is native to laurel forest edges on the Canary Islands and appreciates protection from hot afternoon sun and drying winds. It is damaged below 28°F and thrives in the mild, humid, fog-moderated climates of coastal California and similar maritime conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in part shade; Canary Island foxglove is a tender, shrubby relative of the true foxgloves (Digitalis), native to the laurel cloud forests and scrublands of Tenerife and Gran Canaria in the Canary Islands. Unlike the familiar biennial foxglove, it is an evergreen shrub 3–6 feet tall producing firm, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves and tall upright spikes of densely packed, tubular flowers in a distinctive shade of warm orange-yellow to tawny-amber — one of the few foxglove-family plants producing orange flowers. The flower color is unique among foxglove relatives and is particularly striking in the warm half-tones of the late afternoon light. The flowers are visited by sunbirds in their native habitat and are attractive to hummingbirds in cultivation. Related to Digitalis but classified in the separate genus Isoplexis. A collector's plant of considerable distinction, excellent in mild coastal gardens in zones 9–11 as a specimen in dappled shade.
